preFix.title: Fitting dot-and-dab insulated plasterboard
preFix.subtitle: Fix PIR-backed plasterboard to masonry walls with adhesive dabs
preFix.excerpt: How to fix insulated plasterboard to a masonry wall using the dot-and-dab adhesive method, adding a layer of PIR insulation without battens or a cavity. Covers scanning the wall for services, mixing and applying adhesive dabs, cutting boards to fit, and finishing joints.
preFix.sourceNotes: British Gypsum White Book (installation guides, open-access on manufacturer website). BS 8212: dry lining with gypsum plasterboard. City and Guilds Level 2 Plastering and Drylining. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: Fitting insulated plasterboard by the
dot-and-dab
method fixes boards directly to a masonry wall with gypsum adhesive, adding a layer of
PIR insulation

to the inside face at the same time. No timber battens are involved. The system is faster than building a stud wall but the wall must be reasonably flat: more than 12 mm deviation over 1800 mm and the boards will rock on the dabs and crack the joints. This is a medium-sized job for one person, typically one room in a day.
What you will need: materials

Insulated plasterboard (12.5 mm plasterboard bonded to a PIR foam backing layer, typically 25, 37.5, or 50 mm thick). The 37.5 mm board (25 mm PIR plus 12.5 mm plasterboard) is the standard internal-wall upgrade board. Drylining adhesive (Gyproc Dri-Wall Adhesive or equivalent bonding compound). 13 mm countersunk drywall screws for perimeter mechanical fixings. Jointing compound, 75 mm scrim tape, and 100 mm jointing tape for finishing the joints. A cable-and-pipe detector.
Step 1, check the wall for hidden services

Sweep a cable-and-pipe detector across the entire wall in a grid pattern. Mark the position of any cable or pipe with pencil directly on the masonry. Cables typically run vertically from switches and sockets and horizontally at the 150 mm and 300 mm heights from the floor. Pipes to radiators run either vertically or horizontally from the floor. Where a detector signals a hit, mark a 50 mm exclusion zone on each side. The detector misses deeply embedded services and plastic pipes: if the wall has a history of alterations, widen the exclusion zone to 100 mm.
Step 2, check the wall for flatness

Hold the 1200 mm spirit level flat against the wall in both directions and in several positions. Note any deviation greater than 12 mm over the 1200 mm span. Minor humps can be packed behind the board at that position with an extra dab; depressions of more than 12 mm need to be built up with a bonding coat before the boards go on. A wall with more than 20 mm overall deviation is better lined with a batten system that can be shimmed to a true plane.
Step 3, cut boards to size

Insulated plasterboard is cut in the same way as standard plasterboard: score the face paper and plasterboard skin deeply with a utility knife along a straightedge, snap the board over the knee or a batten, then cut through the back paper. The PIR foam core breaks cleanly across the score line. Cut boards 10 mm short of the floor-to-ceiling height to allow for a gap at the floor and ceiling; this gap is later covered by skirting and cornice. For openings, mark the board face and cut with a jigsaw or panel saw.
Step 4, mix the adhesive

Mix the drylining adhesive to the consistency of stiff peanut butter: it should hold a peak when pulled by the filling knife without slumping. Mix only as much as you can use in 20 minutes; the adhesive sets quickly once mixed. Use a bucket and mix by hand with the 100 mm filling knife. Discard any mixed adhesive that has started to set. Clean the bucket and knife between batches.
Step 5, apply dabs and fix the board

Apply the adhesive in three vertical columns on the back of the board: one near each edge and one down the centre. Within each column, place dabs at approximately 300 mm spacing and around 50 to 60 mm in diameter. Do not apply a continuous bead: the isolated dabs allow air to migrate behind the board as it is pressed, preventing a vacuum seal that would make adjusting the board impossible. At the perimeter, apply a continuous band of adhesive around all four edges, 50 mm wide, to seal against air infiltration at the board edges.

Carry the board to the wall and press it into position. Check plumb and level with the 1200 mm spirit level immediately. The board can be shifted by pressing and rocking it for the first two to three minutes before the adhesive grips. Once level, add three mechanical fixings at the perimeter (screwed into the masonry through the board face within 50 mm of the edges) using 13 mm plasterboard screws and the cordless drill. These fixings hold the board while the adhesive sets and are the required minimum for compliance. Allow the adhesive to set fully before taping the joints: typically 24 hours.
Step 6, tape and fill the joints

Once cured, apply a bed of jointing compound to the joint between boards with the taping knife. Bed 50 mm scrim tape over the joint, pressing it into the compound. Apply a second coat of compound over the tape, feathering it out 150 mm on each side. When dry (typically two hours in a warm room), apply a third coat and feather it out to 250 mm. Sand lightly with the sanding block when fully dry. Internal corners take angle tape; external corners (at a doorway or return wall) take metal angle bead fixed with bonding compound.
Variations

Boards with a 50 mm PIR foam layer give better insulation than the standard 25 mm board. They reduce the room width by more (62.5 mm per wall) and need generous dabs to span the foam-to-masonry gap. On solid stone walls in older houses, a batten system filled with mineral wool is often the better choice: the stone surface is too uneven for reliable dot-and-dab contact.
Watch for
thermal bridges

at reveals (the exposed masonry inside the window opening). These should be lined with a narrow strip of insulated board or a 25 mm PIR board cut to fit. Skipping the reveal lining negates a significant portion of the wall insulation gain.
Care

Insulated plasterboard performs the same as standard plasterboard at the surface and is decorated in the same way. Heavier items fixed to the wall (shelves, radiators, units) must reach through to the masonry behind: plasterboard anchors carry only light loads. Use a long masonry drill bit to locate the masonry through the insulation layer, then plug and fix to the masonry.

attrs.fix: A small hollow area is not structurally dangerous provided the perimeter band is continuous and the mechanical fixings are in place. A large hollow area (more than a quarter of the board) requires the board to be cut out and re-fixed. The perimeter band is the critical seal.
attrs.cause: Insufficient adhesive coverage; air pockets behind the board.
attrs.symptom: Board rings hollow when tapped in the centre after curing

attrs.fix: Allow the full 24-hour cure before taping, and 48 hours in cold or damp conditions. If the joint has cracked through the tape, rake it out, re-tape with fresh compound, and allow to cure before repainting.
attrs.cause: Adhesive not fully cured before taping; board movement while dabs were soft.
attrs.symptom: Joint cracks appear within weeks of decoration

attrs.fix: Remove the board before the adhesive sets fully. Re-apply with consistent dab thickness across the whole board. Check with the spirit level before the adhesive starts to grip.
attrs.cause: Perimeter dabs too thick relative to the centre dabs, pushing the board out of plane.
attrs.symptom: Board bows outward at the centre after fixing

attrs.fix: Line the reveal with a strip of 25 mm PIR board, fixed with adhesive. Tape the joint between the reveal strip and the main board to seal.
attrs.cause: Thermal bridge at the edge of the board where the perimeter adhesive band has not been continued around the reveal.
attrs.symptom: Cold spot or condensation on the finished wall at the board edges

PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
